coredata.dc:title: A label-free electrochemical immunosensor based on the novel signal amplification system of AuPdCu ternary nanoparticles functionalized polymer nanospheres
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Qin
Yang, Yuying
Tan, Zhaoling
Liu, Qing
Liu, Hui
Wang, Ping
Chen, Lei
Zhang, Daopeng
Li, Yueyun
Dong, Yunhui
coredata.dc:description: A sensitive label-free electrochemical immunosensor was designed using a novel signal amplification system for quantitative detecting hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg). Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) supported surfactant-free AuPdCu ternary nanoparticles (AuPdCu/N-GQDs), which featured with good conductivity and excellent catalytic properties for the reduction of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), was synthesized by a simple and benign hydrothermal procedure. At the same time, the electroactive polymer nanospheres (PS) was synthesized by infinite coordination polymers of ferrocenedicarboxylic acid, which could play as carrier and electronic mediator to load AuPdCu/N-GQDs. The PS not only improved the ability to load antibodies because of the good biocompatibility, but also accelerated electron transport of the electrode interface attribute to plentiful ferrocene unit. Thus, the prepared AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS has abilities of good biocompatibility, catalytic activity and electrical conductivity to be applied as transducing materials to amplify electrochemical signal in detection of HBsAg. Under optimal conditions, the fabricated immunosensor exhibited high sensitivity and stability in the detection of HBsAg. A linear relationship between current signals and the concentrations of HBsAg was obtained in the range from 10fg/mL to 50ng/mL and the detection limit of HBsAg was 3.3fg/mL (signal-to-noise ratio of 3). Moreover, the designed immunosensor with excellent selectivity, reproducibility and stability shows excellent performance in detection of human serum samples. Furthermore, this label-free electrochemical immunosensor has promising application in clinical diagnosis of HBsAg.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Label-free electrochemical immunosensor
Polymer nanospheres
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
AuPdCu ternary nanoparticles

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast doctopic grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The mechanism of multiple signal amplification

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic illustration of the label-free electrochemical immunosensor. The preparation procedure of the PS founctionalized with PEI (a), and the fabrication procedure of the AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS (b).

TEM image: (A) N-GQDs, (B) AuPdCu/N-GQDs, (C) PS, (D) AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS; (E) EDX spectrum of AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS; (F) Zeta potential of (a) PS, (b) PEI modified PS, (c) AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS, (d) AuPdCu/N-GQDs.

(A) Amperometric response of different modified electrodes in 10mL PBS (pH = 6.81) with addition of H2O2 (5mol/L, 10μL) at 50s and 100s, respectively: (a) PS, (b) N-GQDs, (c) AuPdCu/N-GQDs, (d) AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS [Inset: response curve of (a) GCE, (b) PS]; (B) A.C. impedance measurement of different materials in a solution containing 0.1mol/L KCl and 2.5mmol/L Fe(CN)6 3−/4− at potential of 0.26V with frequency range of 0.5–105 Hz: (a) bare GCE, (b)AuPdCu/N-GQDs, (c) AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS; (C) A.C. impedance response obtained at the modifies electrode in a solution containing 0.1mol/L KCl and 2.5mmol/L Fe(CN)6 3−/4− at potential of 0.26V with frequency range of 0.5 – 105 Hz: (a) bare GCE, (b) AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS/GCE, (c) anti-HBs/AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS/GCE, (d) BSA/anti-HBs/AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS/GCE, (e) HBsAg/BSA/anti-HBs/AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS/GCE (HBsAg concentration: 1ng/mL).
The optimization of experimental conditions of pH value (A); the AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS concentration (B), error bar = RSD (n = 5).

(A) Amperometric response of the immunosensor to different concentrations of HBsAg, from a to j: 10fg/mL, 50fg/mL, 100fg/mL, 1.0pg/mL, 5.0pg/mL, 50pg/mL, 100pg/mL, 1.0ng/mL, 10ng/mL, 50ng/mL; (B) Calibration curves of immunosensor to different concentrations of HBsAg, error bar = RSD (n = 5).

(A) Amperometric change response of biosensor to five different electrodes treated in same way; (B) The current of interfering substances (100ng/mL) and compound of interfering substances (100ng/mL) and HBsAg (1.0ng/mL); (C) The stability study of the HBsAg immunosensor, error bar = RSD (n = 5).

head.ce:title: A label-free electrochemical immunosensor based on the novel signal amplification system of AuPdCu ternary nanoparticles functionalized polymer nanospheres

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo 255049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sensitive label-free electrochemical immunosensor was designed using a novel signal amplification system for quantitative detecting hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg). Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) supported surfactant-free AuPdCu ternary nanoparticles (AuPdCu/N-GQDs), which featured with good conductivity and excellent catalytic properties for the reduction of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), was synthesized by a simple and benign hydrothermal procedure. At the same time, the electroactive polymer nanospheres (PS) was synthesized by infinite coordination polymers of ferrocenedicarboxylic acid, which could play as carrier and electronic mediator to load AuPdCu/N-GQDs. The PS not only improved the ability to load antibodies because of the good biocompatibility, but also accelerated electron transport of the electrode interface attribute to plentiful ferrocene unit. Thus, the prepared AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS has abilities of good biocompatibility, catalytic activity and electrical conductivity to be applied as transducing materials to amplify electrochemical signal in detection of HBsAg. Under optimal conditions, the fabricated immunosensor exhibited high sensitivity and stability in the detection of HBsAg. A linear relationship between current signals and the concentrations of HBsAg was obtained in the range from 10fg/mL to 50ng/mL and the detection limit of HBsAg was 3.3fg/mL (signal-to-noise ratio of 3). Moreover, the designed immunosensor with excellent selectivity, reproducibility and stability shows excellent performance in detection of human serum samples. Furthermore, this label-free electrochemical immunosensor has promising application in clinical diagnosis of HBsAg.

ce:section.ce:para: Hepatitis B virus (HBV) infection is a major health issues because the infection is extremely common in much of the world and it often leads to chronic infection, cirrhosis and liver cancer (Lavanchy and Kane, 2016; Schweitzer et al., 2015). Hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg) and antibody to HBsAg (anti-HBs) are the first serologic markers to detect HBV infection in people. Meanwhile, HBsAg is a principal marker for the diagnosis of HBV infections (Hassemer et al., 2017; Masson et al., 2017; Shepard et al., 2006). Hence, a highly sensitive, accurate method for determination of HBsAg is of great importance in the early diagnosis of HBV. Up to now, a series of methods have been applied to the quantitative detection of HBsAg. Immunoassays based on the biospecific recognition between the antigens and corresponding antibodies have been widely applied in analytical biochemistry (Zhang et al., 2015), including fluorescence (Pan et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2013), photoelectrochemical (Du et al., 2017), enzyme-linked immunosorbent (Nakayama et al., 2010; Regan et al., 2017), surface-enhanced raman scattering (Chon et al., 2009), electrochemiluminescence (Miao et al., 2016) and electrochemical assays (Cao et al., 2013; Sun et al., 2016). Among these methods, electrochemical immunosensors have attracted considerable attention because of its advantage of low manufacturing cost, rapid detection and high sensitivity (Liu et al., 2016). In particular, label-free electrochemical immunosensor can be performed using miniaturized biosensors due to its simple handling and procedure without a complicated labeling process (Okuno et al., 2007).

In recent research, various materials have been applied in fabricating electrochemical immunosensors, including metallic nanomaterials (Zhang et al., 2016), conductive polymer (Monerris et al., 2016) and graphene quantum dot (Wang et al., 2013). Trimetallic nanomaterials generally possess many characters superior to their single-metal and bimetallic constituents, such as higher electrocatalytic activity and chemical stability (Barman et al., 2018; Gao and Goodman, 2012). Therefore, trimetallic nanomaterials have been applied to immunosensors, opening a new horizon for highly sensitive detection of biomolecules (Liu et al., 2014; Tian et al., 2016). Pd nanoparticles (Pd NPs) is known to have remarkable electrocatalytic activities toward H2O2 (Chen et al., 2012). However, the chemically inert Pd is unstable when exposed to hostile electrochemical environments where the surface Pd atoms dissolved and migrated, leading to aggregation of nanoparticles and reduction of surface area (Hu et al., 2014). Au nanoparticles (Au NPs) as dilution agents integrated with Pd NPs often displays enhanced catalytic stability compared with Pd-alone catalysts, which effectively improves the utilization of Pd NPs (Gao and Goodman, 2012). In addition, Au NPs is regarded as the most stable biocompatible nano-scaled materials (Correard et al., 2014). It is well known that precious metals are expensive and rarely reserved on the earth. In order to keep the high catalytic activity and improve the utility efficiency of noble metal catalysts, alloying of noble metals with non-noble metals (Cu, Fe, Co, Ni etc.) is one of the effective approaches to promote the catalytic activity and reduce the cost of the catalysts (Yang et al., 2011). Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) as a new member of carbon quantum dots in carbon materials, have received enormous attention because of their unique electrical conductivity, electrocatalytic activity combing with low cytotoxicity, excellent water solubility and good biocompatibility (Li et al., 2012a, 2012b; Liu et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2012). Moreover, carbon materials can also serve as reducing agents for the reduction of metal ions to metal nanoparticles, and the N atoms can increase the number of anchoring sites for the adsorption of metal ions (Ju and Chen, 2015). In this work, we use N-GQDs as the support material and reducing agent for the reduction of AuPdCu metal ions to synthesize N-GQDs supported AuPdCu ternary nanoparticles (AuPdCu/N-GQDs).

For electrochemical immunosensors, the ease and speed of the electron transfer affect its sensitivity, and the electronic media plays a significant role in this process. Traditional electronic mediators such as thionine (Ma et al., 2009), methylene blue (Yao et al., 2005), alizarin red (Cao et al., 2016) and ferrocene (Qiu et al., 2009) can not be immobilized on the electrode stably so that the constructed immunosensor is not stable enough. Herein, the electroactive polymer nanospheres (PS) functionalized with polyethylenimine (PEI) based on coordination polymerization of ferrocene derivatives inspired us to synthesize bio-catalytic active nanostructures because of their unique properties of morphology, structural tailorability and biocompatibility. The PEI not only connected the PS with AuPdCu/N-GQDs, but also formed a stable film on the surface of the PS for the immobilization of the electron mediator. As a result, the obtained AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS exhibited a good current signal amplification capacity.

In this study, the PS was synthesized by a simple and green method through photodecomposition and coordination polymerization of ferrocenedicarboxylic acid molecules in methanol. Then, the negative charged AuPdCu/N-GQDs and PS were combined with the positive PEI by electrostatic attraction. The PS acted not only as electron mediator to rapidly transfer electron between the electrode and AuPdCu/N-GQDs, but also as carrier with good water stability to capture the antibodies. Therefore, the designed label-free electrochemical immunosensor has shown its high sensitivity and excellent stability for quantitative detection of HBsAg in human serums.

HBsAg and anti-HBs were purchased from Linc-Bio Science Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA, 96–99%) was purchased from Sigma reagent Co., Ltd. (St. Louis, MO, USA). Ferrocenedicarboxylic acid (Fc-COOH, 98%), polyethylenimine (PEI, M.W. 600, 99%), copric chloride dihydrate (CuCl2·2H2O, 98%) and palladium (II) chloride (PdCl2, 99%) were bought from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Gold (III) chloride hydrate (HAuCl4·4H2O, 99%) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 98%) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30wt%) was bought from Shuangshuang Chemical Co., Ltd (Yantai, China). Citric acid (CA, 99%) and dicyandiamide (DCD, 99%) were purchased from Bodi Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6], 99.95%) was bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) consist of the solution of disodium hydrogen phosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4, 99%) and monopotassium phosphate (KH2PO4, 99.95%) in the process of electrochemical measurements to be the electrolyte. All the experimental processes used ultrapure water (18.25MΩcm, 24°C). All chemicals and solvents were of analytical grade and used as received. The prepared solutions were kept at 4°C prior to use.

The electrochemical measurements were performed on CHI760E electrochemical workstation (China). We used a conventional three-electrode system which included a glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 4mm in diameter) as working electrode, a platinum wire electrode as auxiliary and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as reference electrode in the electrochemical measurements. Zeta potentials were measured through ZS90 microelectrophoresis (United Kingdom). UV–vis absorption spectra were measured on a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (China). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum was collected using Nicolet 5700 FTIR Spectrometric Analyzer (America). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were collected from a Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (America). Energy Dispersive X-Ray spectra (EDX) were recorded by JEOL JSM-6700F microscope (Japan).

The PS was synthesized by infinite coordination polymerization according to the previous literature (Zhang et al., 2015). Typically, 1,1′-ferrocenedicarboxylic acid (Fc-COOH, 5.0mg) was dissolved in 5.0mL methanol to obtain an orange solution. Then the solution was exposed to sunlight for 2h, and the solution color turned to taupe. Under the exposure of natural light and air, Fc-COOH was decomposed into deprotonated generated deprotonated Fc-COO- and Fe3+. Meanwhile, the polymerization occurred between deprotonated Fc-COO- and Fe3+. Subsequently, the precipitate was centrifuged and washed with methanol at least three times. Finally, the polymer nanoparticles was saved in PBS (pH = 6.81) for further usage.

The preparation of N-GQDs was according to the early report (Ju and Chen, 2015). Briefly, 2.0g of CA and 1.0g of DCD were mixed with nanopure water (5.0mL). Then, the mixture was transferred into a 25mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180°C for 12h. Finally, the product was dispersed in 100mL of ultrapure water, and the N-GQDs was collected by removing the large dots through centrifugation at 10,000rpm for 10min.

Firstly, 12.8mg CuCl2·2H2O, 10.6mg PdCl2 and 0.5mL of HAuCl4·4H2O (1.0wt%) were dissolved in 2.5mL of hydrochloric acid, ultrasound for 30min. Then, 5.0mL of N-GQDs was added into the mixture. The pH was adjusted to 10 by NaOH (1.0mol/L), and the mixture was transferred to Teflon-lined autoclave and hydrothermally treated at 180°C for 6h. Solid precipitate appeared in the flask as a result of thermolytic reduction of Cu, Pd, Au and N-GQDs to form AuPdCu/N-GQDs. Eventually, the black precipitate was centrifuged and washed with ultrapure water, and then dried in a vacuum oven at 80°C for 12h.

The AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS was synthesized by self-assembly (Yang et al., 2017). AuPdCu/N-GQDs was used as accessories to modify PS. Firstly, the positive PS was synthesized by mixing 2.0mL PS solution with 1.0mL PEI under shaking for 2h. After centrifugation, 1.0mL suspension solution of AuPdCu/N-GQDs was added into the positive PS solution and shaken for 2h to form the stable structure of AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS by electrostatic attraction. Finally, the prepared AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS was centrifuged and washed with ultrapure water at least three times, and then the precipitate was re-dispersed into PBS solution (pH = 6.81).

In order to illustrate this experimental process clearly, Fig. 1 explained the fabrication of immunosensor. GCE was polished with 0.3 and 0.05µm alumina powder successively, followed by rinsing thoroughly with ultrapure water to obtain fresh and clear surface. Then the polished GCE was covered by AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS nanocomposites (3.0mg/mL, 6.0μL) and dried at room temperature. Next, the integrate AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS/GCE was incubated with anti-HBs (10μg/mL, 6.0μL) and dried at 4°C. After washing, BSA solution (1.0wt%, 3.0μL) was coated onto the surface of electrode to block the nonspecific binding. After 1h incubation, the BSA/anti-HBs/AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS/GCE was washed with PBS solution (pH = 6.81) and incubated with different concentrations of HBsAg (6.0μL) for another 1h at room temperature.

To probe the interface properties of surface-modified electrodes, A.C. impedance measurement was used to record each immobilization steps. The Nyquist plots were recorded from 0.5 to 105 Hz in a solution comprising of 0.1mol/L KCl and 2.5mmol/L Fe(CN)6 3-/4-. Zeta potentials were measured by Nano ZS90 microelectrophoresis. The solution of nanoparticles in PBS (0.1M, pH = 7.0) containing 0.1mol/L KCl as electrolytes was added into a 1.0mL clear zeta potential cuvette. The zeta potential calculated from the measurement of electrophoretic mobility of the sample was collected with Zetasizer software. All of the measurements were replicated three times at room temperature. Amperometric i-t curve was used for HBsAg determination by scanning the potential at − 0.4V in the PBS (0.1M, pH 6.81) solution (Yang et al., 2017). To record the amperometric response, 5.0mM (10μL) H2O2 was added into the PBS (10mL) after the current being stabilized under smooth stirring.

Fig. 2A showed the TEM image of N-GQDs. It can be found that the obtained N-GQDs had the average diameter of 3.0nm and its size distribution was relatively uniform. Besides, the surface chemistry of the N-GQDs was identified by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. S1). After the formation of AuPdCu nanoparticles (AuPdCu NPs) on the N-GQDs, the average size of the AuPdCu/N-GQDs was increased to 20nm. The size and morphology changes clearly indicated that the AuPdCu NPs was successfully loaded on the surface of the presynthesized N-GQDs and the synthesized nanoparticles has good monodispersion and water solubility (Fig. 2B). Additionally, Au, Pd and Cu elements were observed in the EDX of AuPdCu/N-GQDs, which further demonstrated that the AuPdCu NPs was attached on the N-GQDs (Fig. S2F). Moreover, the mapping image of different elements of AuPdCu/N-GQDs (Fig. S2) clearly showed the distribution of Au, Pd and Cu elements, which proved the formation of the ternary nanoparticles structure.

The globular morphology of PS was observed in the TEM image of Fig. 2C and SEM image of Fig. S3A. It could be found that the PS was nanometer-scaled spherical particles with a uniform size of around 120nm. Besides, UV–vis absorption spectrum was conducted to monitor changes before and after the illumination of the Fc-COOH, as displayed in Fig. S4. Before light exposure, Fc-COOH itself showed absorption bands at 224, 255, 306, and 443nm (curve a), which were ascribed to the aromatic ring (224 and 255nm) and the metal-ring charge transfer (306 and 443nm) of Fc-COOH, respectively. Under sunshine for 2h, three absorption peaks for both the aromatic ring (228 and 264nm) and one metal-ring charge transfer (345nm) suggested that the as-formed precipitate contained a ferrocene unit. Meanwhile, the absorption peaks for metal-ring charge transfer (443nm) disappeared due to the photodecomposition of Fc-COOH (Zhang et al., 2015).

An overview TEM image showed that the bright part of PS was surrounded by the dark parts of AuPdCu/N-GQDs, which revealed the morphology of the prepared AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS (Fig. 2D). SEM image confirmed the formation of AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS (Fig. S3B). EDX listing all the elements in the AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS conjugation including C, N, O, Fe, Au, Pd and Cu further confirmed the composition of AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS (Fig. 2E). Moreover, the zeta potentials measurements were recorded to track each step in the process of synthesis. In Fig. 2F, the zeta potential of PS was − 8.7mV, which showed electronegativity attributed to a mass of -COOH. When the PS was combined with positive PEI and then linked to negative AuPdCu/N-GQDs (− 33.0mV), the zeta potential turned into 4.0mV and − 7.5mV, respectively. This process also proved that the AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS conjugates was successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of multiple signal amplification
ce:section.ce:para: The constructed immunosensor has good catalytic activity and conductivity, depending on the synergistic effect of various fabricating materials. For this proposed label-free immunosensors, the sensitivity was mainly determined by the auxiliary catalytic activity of transducing materials towards H2O2 reduction. To compare catalytic performance, the amperometric measurement was employed to record current signals of bare GCE, PS, N-GQDs, AuPdCu/N-GQDs and AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS in PBS (pH = 6.81) containing 5.0mM H2O2. As shown in Fig. 3A, the bare GCE did not show catalytic effect towards the reduction of H2O2 (the inset response of curve a) and the signal increased slightly when the PS was loaded on the electrode (the inset response of curve b). There was an obvious increasing current signal when the N-GQDs was applied to modify the bare GCE (curve b). When the AuPdCu/N-GQDs was modified onto the electrode, there was a considerable current response (curve c), which was ascribed to the promoted electrocatalytic activity of the surfactant-free AuPdCu NPs. After the AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS was modified onto the electrode, the current response was more stable. Hence, the AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS nanocomposites was carried out as signal amplification platform to fabricate a label-free electrochemical immunosensor with high sensitivity.

As reported, A.C. impedance measurement is utilized to monitor the interfacial properties and the changes of the electron transfer resistance (R
et ). The semicircle portion at higher frequencies indicates the electron-transfer limited process and the semicircle diameter corresponds to the R
et (Li et al., 2017). Herein, electrical conductivity of the AuPdCu/N-GQDs and AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS was conducted by A.C. impedance measurement in a solution containing 0.1mol/L KCl and 2.5mmol/L Fe(CN)6 3−/4−. As shown in Fig. 3B, the bare GCE exhibited a smaller R
et (curve a). The A.C. impedance measurement of the AuPdCu/N-GQDs (curve b) showed that the AuPdCu/N-GQDs had good electric conductivity because the intimate electronic interactions between N-GQDs and AuPdCu NPs could improve the electron transfer. The semicircle portion of AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS (curve c) was smaller than AuPdCu/N-GQDs (curve b), indicating that the AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS has better electric conductivity than AuPdCu/N-GQDs. The enhanced electrical conductivity of AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS could be ascribed to that the PS as an electron mediator can significantly improve the electrical conductivity and the electron transfer rate of the electrode. Thus, A.C. impedance measurement confirmed that the AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS nanomaterials has good performance for further construction of the immunosensor.

A.C. impedance measurement was also employed to characterize the fabrication process of the electrochemical immunosensor. As shown in Fig. 3C, the bare GCE (curve a) displayed a well-defined semicircle at high frequencies. The semicircle diameter was decreasing when the AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS (curve b) was dropped onto the surface of GCE, which indicated that the AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS has excellent electric conduction. Subsequently, the semicircle diameter successively increased when the anti-HBs (curve c), BSA (curve d) and HBsAg (curve e) were added layer by layer onto the surface of electrode, indicating the successful modification due to the blocking effect by the modified bioactive substances. Therefore, the A.C. impedance measurement demonstrated that the immunosensor was fabricated successfully.

To obtain the best analytical performance for HBsAg, the experimental conditions including the pH of PBS and the concentration of AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS were optimized. The immunosensor was constructed using HBsAg at a concentration of 1.0ng/mL during this experiment.

The pH of the PBS was investigated under the same concentration of AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS (3.0mg/mL). As seen from Fig. 4A, the current signal increased with the variation of pH value from 5.84 to 6.81 and then significantly decreased with the variation of pH from 6.81 to 8.04, which implied that the pH value obviously affected the electrocatalytic process of AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS towards the reduction of H2O2 and the activity of biological molecular. The result showed that in this construction of immunosensor, AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS displayed enhanced catalytic property towards H2O2 reduction and the biomolecules possessed better biological activity at pH of 6.81. Thus, PBS at pH of 6.81 was chosen as the optimal electrolyte in the following electrochemical experiments.

The concentration of AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS is an important parameter for the performance of immunosensor. Higher or lower concentration of AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS would affect the electron transfer ability and immobilization of antibodies. To obtain the best performance of the immunosensor, the working electrodes modified with series concentrations of AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS were investigated for H2O2 reduction in PBS at pH of 6.81. As shown in Fig. 4B, the amperometric response increased clearly with the increasing concentrations of AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS from 0.5mg/mL to 3.0mg/mL, and then decreased with the concentrations from 3.0mg/mL to 4.0mg/mL. Therefore, the optimal concentration was 3.0mg/mL in this study.

Under the optimal experimental conditions, amperometric i-t curve was used to detect different concentrations of HBsAg using AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS as a signal amplification platform towards the H2O2 reduction in PBS (pH = 6.81). As shown in Fig. 5A, the current signal response of the immunosensor declined along with raising the HBsAg concentration, which should be ascribed to that as the antigen concentration increases, the effect of hindering electron transport increases. The calibration plot in Fig. 5B exhibited a good linear relationship between the current response and the logarithm of the HBsAg concentration in the range from 10 fg/mL to 50ng/mL. The regression equation of the calibration curve was I = − 15.35 lgC + 165.67 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9985. The detection limit of HBsAg was 3.3fg/mL (S/N = 3). This result demonstrated that the constructed immunosensor exhibited the characteristics of high sensitivity, wide detection range and a low detection limit. In addition, the analytical performance of the developed immunosensor for HBsAg detection was compared with other immunosensors reported in the literatures. As summarized in Table S1, the designed immunosensor showed a higher sensitivity and wider linear range, which may be attributed to the following aspects: (1) the AuPdCu/N-GQDs has good electroconductibility and catalytic activity for the reduction of H2O2; (2) the synthetic of AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS with good water solubility can ensure the successful conjugation of anti-HBs.

To evaluate the reproducibility of the immunosensor, five different immunosensors were produced for the detection of 1.0ng/mL of HBsAg ( Fig. 6A). The relative standard deviations (RSD) of the measurements for the five electrodes were 2.4%, which suggested that the reproducibility of the immunosensor was acceptable.

Investigating the specificity of the electrochemical immunosensor was also very important (Fig. 6B). The immunosensors were incubated with 100ng/mL interfering substances, including prostate specific antigen (PSA), human immunoglobulin (IgG), carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) and BSA. The variation of amperometric response of samples with interference and HBsAg was less than 5.0% of that without interferences, which indicated the selectivity of the designed immunosensor was acceptable.

The stability of the prepared immunosensor was inspected by keeping the electrodes at 4°C with the current response being tested per 4 days (Fig. 6C). The maximum and the minimum current response were maintained at 96.38% and 87.35% of the original response, respectively. The result indicated that the designed immunosensor has an acceptable stability, which could be attributed to good biocompatibility of PS and the monodispersity of AuPdCu/N-GQDs.

In order to investigate the precision and feasibility of the label-free electrochemical immunosensor, the recoveries of different concentrations of HBsAg in human serum samples were conducted to detect through the standard addition method. As shown in Table S2, the concentrations of 1.0, 5.0 and 10ng/mL HBsAg solutions were added into serum samples. The RSD was ranged from 2.750% to 1.042% and the recovery rate was from 100.8% to 101.6%. Therefore, the feasibility of the designed immunosensor has a great potential for the clinical determination of the HBsAg levels in serum samples.

To further validate the applicability of the proposed immunosensor, a comparison with the commercialized available ELISA (Goldsmith et al., 1992; Lequin, 2005) method was provided in Table S3. The relative error between the two methods was in the range from − 2.84% to 2.49%. These data demonstrated the feasibility of the proposed immunosensor for clinical application.

In this work, an ultrasensitive label-free electrochemical immunosensor was fabricated successfully and realized the quantitative determination of HBsAg using AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS as a signal amplification platform. The prepared immunosensor based on AuPdCu/N-GQDs@PS could enlarge the captured biomolecules and enhance the catalytic performance to amplify the detection signal. The designed label-free electrochemical immunosensor possessed excellent specificity, good reproducibility and acceptable stability. Therefore, this proposed strategy might have a promising application for other biomolecules in clinical diagnosis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We greatly appreciate the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21405095; 21575079), CPSF (2016M590736) and Special Funding for Postdoctoral Innovation Project of Shandong Province (201501002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.12.040

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Global epidemiology of hepatitis B virus infection
Hepatitis B Virus in Human Diseases
